questions.question: Explain the three types of inheritance in C++ with respect to access control.
keyPoints: Public inheritance: public→public, protected→protected, private→inaccessible
Protected inheritance: public→protected, protected→protected, private→inaccessible
Private inheritance: public→private, protected→private, private→inaccessible
Default for class is private, for struct is public

questions.question: What is the difference between private and protected access specifiers in C++?
keyPoints: Private members accessible only within declaring class and friends
Protected members accessible within class, friends, and derived classes
Protected allows derived classes to access while maintaining encapsulation
This enables inheritance while keeping data hidden from external code

questions.question: Explain the order of constructor and destructor execution in inheritance hierarchy.
keyPoints: Base class constructor executes before derived class constructor
Derived class destructor executes before base class destructor
This ensures proper initialization and cleanup of base parts first
Constructor order: Base → Derived; Destructor order: Derived → Base

questions.question: What is the purpose of access specifiers in OOP?
keyPoints: They implement data encapsulation and information hiding
They control visibility of class members
They help in designing robust class hierarchies
They prevent unauthorized access to internal implementation details

questions.question: Differentiate between 'is-a' and 'has-a' relationships in OOP. Which mechanism implements each?
keyPoints: 'Is-a' represents inheritance relationship (public inheritance)
'Has-a' represents composition/aggregation (member objects)
Example: Dog is a Animal (inheritance); Car has a Engine (composition)
Composition is generally preferred over inheritance for 'has-a' relationships

questions.question: Can constructors and destructors be inherited? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Constructors and destructors are NOT inherited by derived classes
Derived class must define its own constructors/destructor
Derived constructors must explicitly or implicitly call base class constructors
This is because constructors have specific purpose of initializing their own class members

questions.question: What is the default access specifier for class members and class inheritance in C++?
keyPoints: Default access specifier for class members is private
Default inheritance mode for class is private
Default inheritance mode for struct is public
This is an important difference between class and struct in C++

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating all three types of inheritance (public, protected, private) and show how access control works in each case. Include a base class with public, protected, and private members.
keyPoints: Define Base class with all three types of members
Create Derived class with public inheritance - show member accessibility
Create Derived class with protected inheritance - show member accessibility
Create Derived class with private inheritance - show member accessibility
Demonstrate what each derived class can/cannot access
Show that private members remain inaccessible in all cases

questions.question: Explain the concept of protected inheritance with a real-world example. When would you prefer protected inheritance over public inheritance?
keyPoints: Protected inheritance definition and effect on members
Real-world example: Class hierarchy where implementation details should be hidden
Use case: When you want derived classes to use base functionality but not expose it externally
Difference from private inheritance: protected allows further derived classes limited access
Implementation hiding without completely sealing off future extensions

questions.question: Design a class hierarchy for a University system with Person as base class and Student, Professor as derived classes. Discuss the access control decisions for each member variable and function.
keyPoints: Identify members: name, age (protected), salary (private)
Use public inheritance as Student is-a Person, Professor is-a Person
Discuss encapsulation: which data should be protected vs private
Constructor accessibility and inheritance
Consider what external code should and should not access
Apply OOP principles: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism

questions.question: Explain how you would use 'using' declaration to change the access level of inherited members in C++. Provide an example demonstrating this technique.
keyPoints: Syntax: using BaseClass::memberName; in derived class
Purpose: To elevate or demote member accessibility
Example: Making private/protected members accessible as public
Can also be used to avoid name hiding
Restrictions: Cannot change accessibility of constructors directly
Demonstrate with code example showing before and after
